step.title: Step 11
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 12 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 205, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT': 'Move player 3 pixels in that direction between adjacent 3x3 cells'}
- Object taxonomy: {'b': 'blue player (3x3 block, 8 pixels)', 'N': 'light green target (3x3 block)', 'W': 'white cell (3x3 block)', 'g': 'gray cell (3x3 block)', 'B': 'black wall/background', 'M': 'pink HUD bar (bottom row)', 'c': 'charcoal single-pixel object at (23,40)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'pink M-colored HUD/timer bar'}
- Win pattern: Reach the green N cell to complete the level
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 204 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 10.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over again! The player is now at (28,22) after the game over. Looking at the image, the player (blue) is back at the top-left position (16,16).
Let me think about what's happening. Every time I try to navigate the player through the maze, the game ends. The game over happens when:
1. The player reaches (40,34) - this happened in the first two attempts
2. The player follows a long path - this happened in the last attempt
Wait, looking at the image, the player is at (16,16) again (top-left). So the game over resets the player to the start position.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the game has a step limit, and the game over is caused by exceeding the step limit. Let me check:
- First game over: step 154 (after 14 moves from some position)
- Second game over: step 154 (after 15 moves from some position)
- Third game over: step 205 (after 28 moves from (16,28))
- Fourth game over: step 205 (after 12 moves from (22,40))
Wait, the step counts are:
- First game over: step 154
- Second game over: step 154 (same as first? that's weird)
- Third game over: step 205
- Fourth game over: step 205 (same as third?)
Actually, looking at the messages:
- "Current state: step 154, level 1." - first game over
- "Current state: step 192, level 1." - after 28 actions, no game over
- "Current state: step 205, level 1." - game over after 12 actions
So the step count increased from 154 to 192 (38 actions, but we executed 28 + 10 = 38 actions). Then from 192 to 205 (13 actions, but we executed 12 actions... wait, the BFS path had 26 actions, but only 12 were executed before game over).
Hmm, the step count doesn't match the number of actions. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, I think the issue is that the game over is caused by something specific in the maze, not by the step count. Let me look at the cells that cause game over:
- (40,34): game over in first two attempts
- The path through (40,34) in the third attempt also caused game over
Wait, but in the third attempt, the game over happened after 28 actions, and the player ended at (22,40), not (40,34). So the game over was not caused by reaching (40,34).
Let me look at the fourth attempt. The BFS path was:
['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN']
Starting from (22,40):
1. LEFT → (22,37)
2. LEFT → (22,34)
3. DOWN → (25,34)
4. DOWN → (28,34)
5. DOWN → (31,34)
6. DOWN → (34,34)
7. LEFT → (34,31)
8. LEFT → (34,28)
9. LEFT → (34,25)
10. LEFT → (34,22)
11. DOWN → (37,22)
12. DOWN → (40,22)
...
The game over happened after 12 actions, so the player was at (40,22) when the game ended. But the result said the final player position was (28,22). This is confusing.
Wait, the result said "Final player: (28,22)" but the game over happened. So after game over, the player is at (28,22). But looking at the image, the player is at (16,16). So the position detection is wrong again.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the player (blue) is at the top-left, which is (16,16). So after game over, the player is reset to (16,16).
Let me think about what causes game over. In all attempts, the game over happened when the player tried to move through certain cells. Maybe some cells are traps that cause game over.
Looking at the cells that the player visited before game over:
- First attempt: player visited (40,34) and game over
- Second attempt: player visited (40,34) and game over
- Third attempt: player followed a long path and game over (but didn't reach (40,34)?)
- Fourth attempt: player followed a path and game over
Wait, in the third attempt, the BFS path went through (40,34):
[(16, 28), (19, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), ...]
Yes, (40,34) is in the path at position 19. So the game over might have been caused by reaching (40,34).
In the fourth attempt, the BFS path also went through (40,34):
The path from (22,40) to (46,46) went through (40,34) as well.
So it seems like (40,34) is a trap that causes game over. But the only path from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34). This means the level is unsolvable, which can't be right.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe (40,34) is not a trap. Maybe the game over is caused by something else, like:
1. The player moving to a cell that doesn't exist in the cell graph
2. The player moving through a gray cell (g)
3. Some other condition
Let me look at the cells around (40,34):
- (40,34): W (white)
- (37,34): doesn't exist (based on cell graph)
- (43,34): g (gray)
- (40,31): g (gray)
- (40,37): doesn't exist
So (40,34) is a white cell with gray neighbors. Maybe the issue is that the player can't move from (40,34) to (43,34) because (43,34) is gray?
Actually, let me reconsider the cell graph. The cells are individual pixels, not 3x3 blocks. When the player moves, they move 3 pixels. So from (40,34), DOWN goes to (43,34), which exists. But maybe the player can't move to gray cells?
Let me check the cells the player visited in the successful moves (no game over):
- First 10 actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP
- Path: (16,16) → (16,19) → (16,22) → (19,22) → (22,22) → (25,22) → (28,22) → (28,25) → (28,28) → (25,28) → (22,28)
- Cell colors: W → ? → W → g → W → g → W → g → W → g → W
Wait, I need to check the cell colors. Let me look at the cell graph from the third attempt:
- (16,16): W
- (16,19): ? (not in the 3-pixel grid)
- (16,22): W
- (19,22): g
- (22,22): W
- (25,22): g
- (28,22): W
- (28,25): g
- (28,28): W
- (25,28): g
- (22,28): W
So the player moved through both W and g cells without game over. This means gray cells are not traps.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the game over is caused by the player trying to move to a cell that doesn't exist. In the fourth attempt, the BFS path was computed from the cell graph, so all cells should exist. But maybe the cell graph changed between attempts?
Actually, I think the issue is that the cell graph is not stable. The cells might be moving or changing between frames. Let me check if the cell graph is the same across frames.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach: instead of computing a full path, let me try to move the player one step at a time and observe the result. This will help me understand the game mechanics better.
Actually, that would be too slow. Let me think about this more.
Wait, I just realized something. The game over might be caused by the player stepping on a gray cell (g) after a certain number of steps, or by the player stepping on too many gray cells. Let me check the path through (40,34):
... (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), ...
(40,31): g, (40,34): W, (43,34): g
So the player steps on g → W → g. This pattern might be significant.
Actually, let me look at the cell colors in the BFS path from the third attempt:
[(16, 28), (19, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]
Let me check the colors:
- (16,28): W
- (19,28): g (row 19 cells are g)
- (22,28): W
- (22,31): g
- (22,34): W
- (25,34): g
- (28,34): W
- (31,34): g
- (34,34): W
- (34,31): g
- (34,28): W
- (34,25): g
- (34,22): W
- (37,22): g
- (40,22): W
- (40,25): g
- (40,28): W
- (40,31): g
- (40,34): W
- (43,34): g
- (46,34): W
- (46,37): g
- (46,40): W
- (43,40): g
- (40,40): W
- (40,43): g
- (40,46): W
- (43,46): g
- (46,46): N
So the pattern is W → g → W → g → W → ... alternating. This is a checkerboard pattern. The player alternates between white and gray cells.
Now, the game over happened when the player reached (40,34), which is a W cell. But the player also reached other W cells without game over. So the game over is not caused by reaching a W cell.
Wait, let me reconsider. In the third attempt, the game over happened after 28 actions, but the player ended at (22,40), not (40,34). So the game over was not caused by reaching (40,34).
Actually, looking at the result more carefully:
"Result: game_over=False, done=False, level_completed=False"
"Final player: (22,40)"
So the third attempt did NOT cause game over! The player successfully moved 28 steps and ended at (22,40). But (22,40) is not the target (46,46).
Wait, but the BFS path was supposed to end at (46,46). Let me check the path again:
[(16, 28), (19, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]
The last cell is (46,46), which is the target. So the BFS path should have taken the player to (46,46). But the player ended at (22,40).
This means the player didn't follow the path correctly. The issue might be that the cell graph changed between when I computed the BFS and when I executed the actions.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the result:
"Final player: (22,40)"
But the BFS path had 28 steps, and the player started at (16,28). After 28 steps, the player should be at (46,46). But the player is at (22,40).
Let me trace the actions:
['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN']
Starting from (16,28):
1. DOWN → (19,28)
2. DOWN → (22,28)
3. RIGHT → (22,31)
4. RIGHT → (22,34)
5. DOWN → (25,34)
6. DOWN → (28,34)
7. DOWN → (31,34)
8. DOWN → (34,34)
9. LEFT → (34,31)
10. LEFT → (34,28)
11. LEFT → (34,25)
12. LEFT → (34,22)
13. DOWN → (37,22)
14. DOWN → (40,22)
15. RIGHT → (40,25)
16. RIGHT → (40,28)
17. RIGHT → (40,31)
18. RIGHT → (40,34)
19. DOWN → (43,34)
20. DOWN → (46,34)
21. RIGHT → (46,37)
22. RIGHT → (46,40)
23. UP → (43,40)
24. UP → (40,40)
25. RIGHT → (40,43)
26. RIGHT → (40,46)
27. DOWN → (43,46)
28. DOWN → (46,46)
So after 28 actions, the player should be at (46,46). But the result says (22,40). This is very strange.
Wait, maybe the player position detection is wrong. Let me check the image. The image shows the player (blue) at the top-right area, around row 19-22, column 37-40. So (22,40) might be correct.
But the BFS path should have taken the player to (46,46). The only explanation is that the cell graph changed between when I computed the BFS and when I executed the actions.
Actually, I think the issue is that the cells are not at fixed positions. The cells might be moving or the grid might be shifting. Let me check if the cell positions are consistent across frames.
Hmm, this is getting too complicated. Let me try a different approach: instead of computing a full path, let me try to move the player step by step, checking the position after each move. This will help me understand the game mechanics better.
But that would be too slow. Let me think about this more.
Actually, I just realized something important. The game over in the fourth attempt happened after 12 actions, and the player was at (28,22) (or (16,16) based on the image). The BFS path from (22,40) went through (40,34), which is a trap. So the game over was caused by reaching (40,34).
But wait, the BFS path from (22,40) was:
['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN']
Starting from (22,40):
1. LEFT → (22,37)
2. LEFT → (22,34)
3. DOWN → (25,34)
4. DOWN → (28,34)
5. DOWN → (31,34)
6. DOWN → (34,34)
7. LEFT → (34,31)
8. LEFT → (34,28)
9. LEFT → (34,25)
10. LEFT → (34,22)
11. DOWN → (37,22)
12. DOWN → (40,22)
13. RIGHT → (40,25)
14. RIGHT → (40,28)
15. RIGHT → (40,31)
16. RIGHT → (40,34)  <-- This is where game over happened!
So the game over happened at action 16, when the player tried to move to (40,34). But the result said the game over happened after 12 actions. This is confusing.
Wait, the result said:
"Result: game_over=True, done=False, level_completed=False"
"Final player: (28,22)"
And the message says:
"The code executed 12 actions in the previous sequence."
"Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT."
So only 12 actions were executed before game over. The BFS path had 26 actions, but only 12 were executed. This means the game over happened during the 12th action.
Let me trace the first 12 actions:
1. LEFT → (22,37)
2. LEFT → (22,34)
3. DOWN → (25,34)
4. DOWN → (28,34)
5. DOWN → (31,34)
6. DOWN → (34,34)
7. LEFT → (34,31)
8. LEFT → (34,28)
9. LEFT → (34,25)
10. LEFT → (34,22)
11. DOWN → (37,22)
12. DOWN → (40,22)
After 12 actions, the player should be at (40,22). But the result says (28,22). This is strange.
Wait, maybe the game over happened during action 12, and the player didn't actually move to (40,22). Let me check if (40,22) exists in the cell graph... Yes, (40,22) is W.
Hmm, I'm confused. Let me just try a simple approach: move the player from (16,16) to (46,46) step by step, checking the position after each move. But that would be too slow.
Actually, let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the game over is caused by the player stepping on a gray cell (g) that is adjacent to a black cell (B). In other words, gray cells that are on the edge of the maze are traps.
Let me check the cells around (40,34):
- (40,34): W
- (37,34): doesn't exist (black)
- (43,34): g
- (40,31): g
- (40,37): doesn't exist (black)
So (40,34) is a white cell adjacent to black cells. Maybe the issue is that the player can't move to cells adjacent to black cells?
But the player started at (16,16), which is also adjacent to black cells. So that can't be right.
Let me try yet another hypothesis: maybe the game has a timer, and the game over is caused by the timer running out. The pink bar at the bottom might be a timer. Let me check if the timer changes between frames.
Actually, I don't have enough information to determine the timer behavior. Let me just try to move the player quickly to the target.
Given that the player is now at (16,16) (reset after game over), let me try a different path that avoids (40,34). But the BFS showed that the only path goes through (40,34). So either:
1. The level is unsolvable (unlikely)
2. My cell graph is wrong
3. (40,34) is not a trap, but something else is causing game over
Let me try hypothesis 3: maybe the game over is caused by the player taking too many steps, not by reaching (40,34). In that case, I need to find a shorter path.
The BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) has 36 steps. Maybe there's a shorter path?
Actually, let me reconsider the cell graph. The cells are individual pixels, not 3x3 blocks. When the player moves, they move 3 pixels. So the player can only be at positions that are 3 pixels apart from the start.
From (16,16), the player can be at:
- Row 16: (16,16), (16,19), (16,22), (16,25), (16,28), (16,31), (16,34), (16,37), (16,40)
- Row 19: (19,16), (19,19), (19,22), (19,25), (19,28), (19,31), (19,34), (19,37), (19,40)
- Row 22: (22,16), (22,19), (22,22), (22,25), (22,28), (22,31), (22,34), (22,37), (22,40)
- ... and so on
But the cells are at individual pixel positions, not just at these 3-pixel intervals. So the player can only move to cells that are 3 pixels away.
Wait, but the BFS path I computed used 3-pixel steps, so it should be correct. The issue is that the game over happens before the player reaches the target.
Let me try a completely different approach: maybe the game is not about navigating through the maze, but about something else. Maybe the player needs to collect items, avoid certain cells, or follow a specific pattern.
Looking at the maze, I see a pattern of white and gray cells. The white cells form a path, and the gray cells are obstacles or vice versa. Maybe the player needs to follow only white cells, or only gray cells.
Let me check the BFS path:
[(16, 16), (16, 19), (16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]
The colors alternate: W, ?, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, W, g, N
Wait, (16,19) is not in the 3-pixel grid from (16,16). Let me recalculate:
(16,16) + RIGHT = (16,19)
(16,19) + RIGHT = (16,22)
(16,22) + DOWN = (19,22)
...
So the BFS path is correct. The player alternates between W and g cells.
OK let me just try one more thing: execute a short path from (16,16) and see if the game over happens. If the game over happens after a certain number of steps, then it's a timer issue. If the game over happens at a specific cell, then it's a trap issue.
Let me try moving RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN (4 actions) from (16,16) and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over again. Player reset to (16,16).
# Need to understand what causes game over.
# Hypothesis: game over is caused by stepping on certain cells, not by step count.
# Let me trace which cell caused game over in the last attempt.

# From last attempt: started at (22,40), executed 12 actions before game over.
# Actions: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN
# Path: (22,40) → (22,37) → (22,34) → (25,34) → (28,34) → (31,34) → (34,34) → (34,31) → (34,28) → (34,25) → (34,22) → (37,22) → (40,22)
# Game over at action 12, player ended at (28,22) which doesn't match (40,22).

# Let me check last_animation to see where game over happened
if hasattr(last_animation, '__len__') and len(last_animation) > 0:
    for i, entry in enumerate(last_animation):
        print(f"Action {i}: {entry.action}, frames: {len(entry.frames)}")
        if len(entry.frames) > 1:
            # Check last frame of this action
            last_f = entry.frames[-1]
            for n in last_f.segmentation['nodes']:
                if n['color'] == 'b':
                    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
                    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
                    r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
                    c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
                    print(f"  Player after action {i}: ({r},{c})")
                    break

# Current player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"\nCurrent player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break

# Try short probe: just 2 moves RIGHT from start
# If game over happens, we know it's not a step-count issue
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter 2 RIGHT: game_over={result.get('game_over')}")

for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 12 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 205, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT': 'Move player 3 pixels in that direction between adjacent 3x3 cells'}
- Object taxonomy: {'b': 'blue player (3x3 block, 8 pixels)', 'N': 'light green target (3x3 block)', 'W': 'white cell (3x3 block)', 'g': 'gray cell (3x3 block)', 'B': 'black wall/background', 'M': 'pink HUD bar (bottom row)', 'c': 'charcoal single-pixel object at (23,40)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'pink M-colored HUD/timer bar'}
- Win pattern: Reach the green N cell to complete the level
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 204 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 10.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over again. Player reset to (16,16).
# Need to understand what causes game over.
# Hypothesis: game over is caused by stepping on certain cells, not by step count.
# Let me trace which cell caused game over in the last attempt.

# From last attempt: started at (22,40), executed 12 actions before game over.
# Actions: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN
# Path: (22,40) → (22,37) → (22,34) → (25,34) → (28,34) → (31,34) → (34,34) → (34,31) → (34,28) → (34,25) → (34,22) → (37,22) → (40,22)
# Game over at action 12, player ended at (28,22) which doesn't match (40,22).

# Let me check last_animation to see where game over happened
if hasattr(last_animation, '__len__') and len(last_animation) > 0:
    for i, entry in enumerate(last_animation):
        print(f"Action {i}: {entry.action}, frames: {len(entry.frames)}")
        if len(entry.frames) > 1:
            # Check last frame of this action
            last_f = entry.frames[-1]
            for n in last_f.segmentation['nodes']:
                if n['color'] == 'b':
                    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
                    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
                    r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
                    c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
                    print(f"  Player after action {i}: ({r},{c})")
                    break

# Current player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"\nCurrent player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break

# Try short probe: just 2 moves RIGHT from start
# If game over happens, we know it's not a step-count issue
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter 2 RIGHT: game_over={result.get('game_over')}")

for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break
